1.description: Exposure measure over time This figure presents the time trend of our main independent variable of interest, Exposure, which is defined as the ratio of a bank’s short-term wholesale funding to its liabilities. The figure plots an average of the Exposure variable across the banks in our sample in each quarter. The dashed vertical line represents the timing of the short-term wholesale funding shock event.

2.description: Credit card limits extended within-individual over time This figure plots the differences in credit card limits extended by different banks with varying exposure to the short-term wholesale funding shock to the same individual. The figure plots the coefficients associated with Exposure using a specification similar to Equation (1) with individual fixed-effects estimated separately for each archive. The change in credit limits for each archive is computed relative to the January 2008 archive (i.e., the omitted category). The points represent coefficient estimates, and the solid vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients. The dashed vertical line represents the timing of the short-term wholesale funding shock event.

3.description: Estimation excluding each bank This figure shows the series of coefficients (β) associated with Exposure from Equation (1), which is estimated by excluding each BHC shown on the x-axis. The circles represent the coefficient estimate β. The solid vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients. The point estimates are ordered based on bank size. Thus, “Bank 1” represents the coefficient estimate after removing the largest bank in our sample, and “Bank 18” represents the coefficient estimate after removing the smallest bank in our sample.

4.description: Effect of funding shock on credit-card-level limits: Heterogeneity across consumer segments This figure shows how banks’ preshock dependence on short-term wholesale funding affects the change in credit-card-level limits across different consumer segments. In panel A, consumers are grouped into 10-point credit utilization bins. In panel B, consumers are grouped into 20-point credit score bins. The circles show the coefficient β associated with Exposure from Equation (1), and the solid vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals associated with the coefficients (left vertical axis). The triangles identify the percentage change in credit card limits relative to the average preshock card-level limit for the consumers in each bin (right vertical axis).

5.description: Effect of funding shock on individual-level credit card limits: Heterogeneity across consumer segments This figure shows how the relationship between consumers’ exposure to their banks’ funding shock and their total credit card limits varies across different consumer segments. In panel A, consumers are grouped into 10-point credit utilization bins. In panel B, consumers are grouped into 20-point credit score bins. The coefficient estimates obtained by regressing the change in individual-level limits on the Weighted exposure measure using Equation (2) are represented as circles, and the solid vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals associated with the coefficients (left vertical axis). The triangles represent the percentage change in credit card limits relative to the average preshock individual-level limit for the consumers in each bin (right vertical axis).

6.description: Effect of funding shock on individual-level credit card balances: Heterogeneity across consumer segments This figure shows how consumers’ exposure to their banks’ funding shock affects their total credit card balances across different consumer segments. In panel A, consumers are grouped into 10-point credit utilization bins. In panel B, consumers are grouped into 20-point credit score bins. The coefficient estimates are obtained by regressing the change in individual-level credit card balances on the Weighted exposure measure using a specification similar to Equation (2). The coefficient estimates are represented as circles, and the solid vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals associated with the coefficients. The triangles represent the percentage change in credit card balance relative to the average preshock individual-level credit card balance for the consumers in each bin (right vertical axis).

7.description: Long-run effect of the funding shock on bank credit supply This figure plots the total credit card limits extended by the high-exposure (above-median Exposure) and low-exposure (below-median Exposure) banks relative to the January 2008 archive. The total credit limits for each group are computed by aggregating the credit limits extended across all open credit cards issued by the banks in each group in that particular year.

8.description: Long-run effect of the funding shock on individual-level credit card limits and balances This figure plots the long-run effect of the funding shock on individual-level credit card limits and balances. These point estimates are estimated using Equation (3) for prime (⁠≥ 680) and nonprime (< 680) consumers, as classified in the preshock period. The change in credit card limits and the change in credit card balances for each archive are computed relative to the January 2008 archive (i.e., the omitted category). The points represent coefficient estimates, and the dashed vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients.

9.description: Long-run effect of the funding shock on credit card limits: Exposed banks versus all banks This figure plots the long-run effect of the funding shock on individual-level credit card limits using Equation (3). Triangles represent these effects for credit limits aggregated across all cards. Circles represent these effects for credit limits aggregated over the cards issued only by the exposed banks. These point estimates are estimated separately for nonprime (< 680) consumers in panel A and prime (⁠≥ 680) consumers in panel B, as classified in the preshock period. The change in credit card limits for each archive is computed relative to the January 2008 archive (i.e., the omitted category). The points represent coefficient estimates, and the dashed vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients.

10.description: Long-run effect of the funding shock on credit card balances: Exposed banks versus all banks This figure plots the long-run effect of the funding shock on individual-level credit card balances using Equation (3). Triangles represent these effects for credit balances aggregated across all cards. Circles represent these effects for credit balances aggregated over the cards issued only by the exposed banks. These point estimates are estimated separately for nonprime (< 680) consumers in panel A and prime (⁠≥ 680) consumers in panel B, as classified in the preshock period. The change in credit card balances for each archive is computed relative to the January 2008 archive (i.e., the omitted category). The points represent coefficient estimates, and the dashed vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients.

11.description: Credit balance dynamics of high- and low-exposure individuals This figure plots the long-run credit card balance dynamics of high-exposure (circles) and low-exposure (triangles) individuals by aggregating across all the cards of an individual. Panel A plots credit card balances divided by preshock credit card limits in 2008 (event year 0), that is, Balancet/Limit0⁠. Panel B plots the credit utilization ratio, that is, Balancet/Limitt⁠. Individuals are sorted into 10 nonoverlapping equal-width credit utilization bins (i.e., 0–0.1, 0.1–0.2,…⁠, 0.9–1) based on their preshock credit utilization in 2008. Subsequently, individuals are categorized into high- and low-exposure individuals based on the median Weighted exposure measure.

12.description: Effect of funding shock on consumer fundamentals This figure shows the credit scores and modeled income of consumers over time based on their exposure to the short-term wholesale funding shock. Modeled income is an estimate of consumer income provided by the credit bureau. Both panels plot the coefficient estimates on the Weighted exposure measure using specifications similar to Equation (3) with the change in credit scores as the dependent variable in panel A, and the change in modeled income as the dependent variable in panel B. The point estimates are estimated for prime (⁠≥ 680) and nonprime (< 680) consumers, as classified in the preshock period. The points represent coefficient estimates, and the dashed vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients.

13.description: Testing path dependence in credit card limits This figure plots the relationship between individual-level credit limits in the post-shock period and the individual-level credit limits in the first year immediately after the funding shock. These point estimates are estimated using Equation (4) for prime (⁠≥ 680) and nonprime (< 680) consumers, as classified in the preshock period. The points represent coefficient estimates, and the dashed vertical lines represent the 95% confidence intervals for the estimated coefficients.
